text: Welcome to our presentation on detecting safety violations across many agent traces.
My name is Adam Stein, and I co-authored this work with my colleagues listed here at the University of Pennsylvania.
This research is based on our recent arXiv preprint from June 2026.
Current AI safety auditing mostly examines individual agent traces for failures.
However, real-world violations often span multiple traces, revealing complex failure modes when analyzed collectively.
For example, ransomware attacks appear benign in single traces but reveal coordinated exfiltration patterns across many.
Challenges include rarity, adversarial disguise, and missed detection by per-trace monitors.
Therefore, we need novel auditing approaches that identify violations across many traces simultaneously.
This figure illustrates our motivating example with a Meerkat agentic search.
Individual traces show safe actions such as recovery instructions and maintenance drafts.
However, subsets of these traces, when combined, reveal a coordinated ransomware attack pattern involving key exfiltration.
This adversarial misuse is invisible per trace but evident cross-trace as a violating witness W.
Previous work includes trace-level monitoring for sabotage and deception, but with limitations.
Automated auditing through red teaming exists but often targets individual traces.
Corpus-scale analysis helps discovery but relies on human-in-the-loop methods.
Most methods miss complex hyperproperty failures across traces, a key limitation we address.
Formal methods and runtime verification do not scale well nor handle natural-language properties.
Our goal is to audit a repository of agent traces for safety violations specified by natural-language properties.
Traditional methods classify traces independently, but OurMethod searches for violating sets or witnesses of traces.
OurMethod involves embedding traces, clustering to reveal related groups, and building an agent prompt to combine these.
We then use an agent reasoning system to propose witnesses, score traces, and emit verdicts: PASS, FAIL, or INCONCLUSIVE.
This approach localizes and detects failures invisible to per-trace analysis.
